talk_metadata.talk_title: Functional significance of M6A readers YTHDF1/2/3 in acute myeloid leukemia
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: we haven't looked into that.
Speaker said: I don't have a clue.

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: low potency and lack of selectivity for YTHDF2 versus other YTHDF paralogs
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted the compound is "very much compound in optimization" and did not present toxicity data

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: YTHDF2 inhibitor scaffold with IC50 11 µM
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed the structure cannot be shown for IP reasons, indicating limited differentiation from existing tool compounds
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Pursuing monotherapy despite data suggesting need for combination with Ara‑C

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that YTHDF2 deletion expands normal hematopoietic stem cells (no phenotype) yet concluded that YTHDF2 inhibition will be a safe therapeutic strategy without presenting long‑term toxicity data

key_data_gaps: No in vivo efficacy data for the small‑molecule inhibitor
No toxicity assessment in non‑hematopoietic tissues
No biomarker‑stratified analysis of patient samples beyond TP53 status

crispro_opportunity.description: TP53‑mutant AML appears especially sensitive to YTHDF3 knockdown, but the effect in TP53‑wildtype AML is unclear
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "Mutant AML seems to be very sensitive... TP53 doesn't segregate responders and non‑responders"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO could develop a TP53‑status‑linked predictive model to select patients for YTHDF‑targeted therapy

crispro_opportunity.description: YTHDF3 deletion sensitizes AML cells to Ara‑C, suggesting a combination strategy
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "YTHDF3 deletion sensitized to Ara‑C"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model synergistic drug‑drug interactions between YTHDF inhibitors and standard chemotherapy

crispro_opportunity.description: Current YTHDF2 inhibitor has low potency; computational redesign could improve affinity and selectivity
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "its IC50 is only 11 micromolar, so it's very much compound in optimization"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO's structure‑based design platform can optimize the scaffold for sub‑micromolar potency

unresolved_questions: What mechanisms underlie resistance to YTHDF inhibition in AML?
Do YTHDF mutations occur in patient cohorts and affect drug response?
What is the impact of YTHDF inhibition on non‑hematopoietic tissues in long‑term studies?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: University of Chicago, Department of Chemistry
talk_metadata.talk_title: M6A readers in tumor microenvironment and chromatin regulation
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We haven't done that much R‑loop correlation with M6A"
Speaker said: "I won't be surprised, there's a correlation"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Limited in‑vivo potency and lack of pharmacokinetic data for solid‑tumor models

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: YTHDC1 inhibitor scaffold (publicly available tool compound)
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker used a publicly disclosed compound from a preprint, indicating low barrier to entry for competitors
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on a single inhibitor without demonstrated clinical‑grade optimization

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented extensive preclinical data showing YTHDC1 inhibition collapses stemness in neuroendocrine cancers, yet concluded that the same inhibitor "gave us the best result" without providing comparative pharmacodynamic or safety data

key_data_gaps: No pharmacokinetic or toxicity data for YTHDC1 inhibitor
Absence of clinical trial or patient‑derived data
Limited mechanistic insight into why YTHDC1 acts as activator in solid tumors versus repressor in other contexts

crispro_opportunity.description: Unclear whether YTHDC1 inhibition affects DNA damage response pathways (e.g., ATM, PARP) in solid tumors
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "I won't be surprised, there are several studies suggesting YTHDF2 may be involved in DNA damage repair"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate epigenomic and DNA‑damage response datasets to predict combinatorial strategies with PARP inhibitors

crispro_opportunity.description: YTHDC1 binds promoter‑proximal lncRNAs to regulate chromatin; CrisPRO's RNA‑structure prediction could identify additional druggable RNA‑protein interfaces
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "YTHDC1 binds M6A at LOO elements and activates transcription"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Leverage CrisPRO's computational spine to model YTHDC1‑RNA interactions and design higher‑affinity disruptors

crispro_opportunity.description: Current preclinical studies lack biomarker‑driven patient selection; incorporating YTHDC1 expression or LOO‑element methylation could enrich for responders
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We tested four different preclinical models... YTHDC1 inhibition depletes the cycling population"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO to develop a predictive biomarker panel (YTHDC1 levels, LOO‑element methylation) for future early‑phase trials

unresolved_questions: Does YTHDC1 inhibition modulate homologous recombination or other DNA‑damage repair pathways?
What post‑translational modifications of YTHDC1 dictate its switch from repressor to activator?
Are there synergistic effects of YTHDC1 inhibitors with radiation or immune checkpoint blockade?
